attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two day history of a rapidly worsening rash and blistering. The rash is causing severe pain, with a burning sensation over the affected areas. The patient is normally in good health, but two weeks ago started taking diclofenac for a work-related back injury.

On examination his blood pressure is 116/70 mmHg, pulse 98/min and temperature 38.8oC. He was a widespread rash (Image) over about 50% of his body. The mucous membranes, including the oral cavity and conjunctiva, are affected with blister formation. There is extensive epidermal detachment and Nikolsky's sign is positive. There are no other remarkable findings on examination.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This is a case of toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN), an uncommon, severe and potentially life-threatening, dermatological emergency causing cutaneous reaction characterised by widespread epidermal detachment and blister formation. This condition is a type 4 hypersensitivity reaction, most commonly to a drug cause, and rarely has been reported to occur secondary to infections. Many different drugs are known to have caused TEN, but most often those involved are antibiotics, anti-epileptic drugs and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory agents and the problem tends to arise within the first few weeks of starting a new medication. The presentation typically includes rapidly progressing erythema, pain, and burning sensation over a large body surface area, often exceeding 30%. The mucous membranes, such as the oral cavity and conjunctiva, are frequently involved. Nikolsky's sign, which refers to the separation of the epidermis from the underlying dermis with minimal pressure or rubbing, is usually positive in TEN. The other pathognomonic feature on examination is the Asboe-Hansen sign, which is the extension of the blister when applying pressure laterally.

Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) is a less severe variant of TEN and shares similar features, including widespread erythema, blister formation, and mucous membrane involvement. However, SJS typically affects less than 10% of the body surface area, whereas TEN involves more than 30%. Therefore, SJS is not the most likely diagnosis in this case.

Drug-induced hypersensitivity syndrome (DIHS), also known as drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms, is a severe cutaneous adverse reaction to medications. It typically presents with fever, rash, lymphadenopathy, and multiorgan involvement. While DIHS can cause skin blistering, the widespread epidermal detachment seen in TEN is not a characteristic feature of DIHS. Therefore, DIHS is not the most likely diagnosis in this case.

Bullous pemphigoid (BP) is an autoimmune blistering disorder that predominantly affects older individuals. It is characterised by tense blisters on the skin. It can sometimes present acutely if there is a drug-related cause. Nikolsky's sign can be useful in helping to differentiate between pemphigus vulgaris (present) from bullous pemphigoid (absent).

Erythema multiforme (EM) is an acute hypersensitivity reaction that characteristically presents with target-like skin lesions that are multiple and erythematous and sometimes may blister. It is commonly associated with infections, particularly herpes simplex virus (HSV) infection, drugs or systemic diseases. EM is an important ddx for SJS/TEN and can sometimes be very hard to differentiate clinically without a biopsy but is usually differentiated on the basis of clinical history.

Pemphigus vulgaris (PV) is an autoimmune blistering disorder characterised by flaccid bullae and erosions on the skin and mucous membranes. PV typically presents with isolated or grouped lesions rather than widespread epidermal detachment.
learningPoints: TEN is a severe and life-threatening skin reaction with widespread blistering and mucous membrane involvement, often triggered by medication.
references: Labib AM, Milroy C. Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis. 2023 May 8. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan–. PMID: 34662044.
Pereira FA, Mudgil AV, Rosmarin DM. Toxic epidermal necrolysis. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2007 Feb;56(2):181-200. doi: 10.1016/j.jaad.2006.04.048. PMID: 17224365.
